macro_segments.title: Learning the Firstborn Mother-Child Appearance Principle from Ox, Sheep, and Goat

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Firstborn animals must match their mother's appearance
Rabbis' derivation: Learning from three animals via Klal U'Prat (General and Particular)
Rabbi Yose HaGlili's alternative: Double mention of Bechor teaches mother-child rule

macro_segments.title: Applying the Mother-Child Rule: Donkey Firstborn Limited to Donkeys Alone

micro_segments.title: Broad verse narrowed: From all non-kosher to only donkeys

macro_segments.title: Learning the Firstborn Mother-Child Appearance Principle from Ox, Sheep, and Goat (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Achai's challenge: Logical rules for inclusions and exclusions
Gemara's response: Why say Bechor twice if context is already clear?

macro_segments.title: Applying the Mother-Child Rule: Donkey Firstborn Limited to Donkeys Alone (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tanna's derivation: Peter Chamor mentioned twice excludes horses and camels
Rav Pappa's alternative: Klal U'Prat with all your animals limits to three species
Rabbi Yose's objection: Second verse breaks the Klal U'Prat connection
Rabbis' response: Vav connection and different sanctity types justify separation

macro_segments.title: Partial Simanim (Characteristics): When Does Child Resemble Mother Enough?

micro_segments.title: Case posed: Cow births animal resembling donkey with some cow characteristics
Comparison to sheep-goat cases: Same parsha, same kedusha type
Question unresolved: Different categories (kosher/non-kosher, kedusha types)
Baraita evidence: Partial simanim obligates in some non-kosher cases
Why mention donkey-horse case: To teach that partial donkey traits suffice
Unresolved: Horse and horse cases with partial mother characteristics

macro_segments.title: Parentage and Species Determination: Mother versus Appearance

micro_segments.title: General principle: What comes from non-kosher is non-kosher
Mishnah framework: Offspring inherits mother's status in most cases
Rabban Gamliel's view: Appearance alone determines kosher status
Rabbis' view: Mother determines status regardless of appearance
Camel twice teaches: Both animal and byproducts (milk) forbidden
Rabbi Shimon: Camel mention teaches mother AND byproducts forbidden

macro_segments.title: The Principle Yotzei Min HaTamei Tamei: Non-Kosher Byproducts Are Forbidden

micro_segments.title: Why teach milk separately: Milk of kosher animals is itself a novelty
Blood transformed to milk: Why milk from kosher animal is allowed at all
Milk not an eiver min hachai: Not technically a limb of living animal
Proof milk is permitted: From redeemed sacrificial animals' milk rules
Biblical support: Promised land flows with milk, David brings cheese
Conclusion: Even non-kosher animal byproducts (milk) are forbidden

topical_tags.term: Yotzei Min HaTamei Tamei (offspring of non-kosher are non-kosher)
Eiver Min HaChai (limb from living animal)
